commit.message:
fix: resolve PR validation failures — GHAS audit, Gitleaks replacement, JaCoCo, permissions (#12)

* Initial plan

* fix: add continue-on-error to dependency-review and secrets-scan jobs

Dependency Review fails because GitHub Advanced Security (GHAS) is
required for private repos in addition to Dependency graph being enabled.
Secrets Scan fails because GITLEAKS_LICENSE secret is not yet set for
the organisation. Both jobs now use continue-on-error: true so they
don't block PR merges while these prerequisites are being provisioned.
Security checks still run and post results; they just no longer gate
merges until GHAS and the licence key are in place.

Co-authored-by: Shwelinhtet-PaloIT <257685547+Shwelinhtet-PaloIT@users.noreply.github.com>

* fix: resolve all PR validation CI failures

GHAS audit results:
- Dependency Graph: ENABLED (user confirmed)
- GitHub Advanced Security: NOT enabled (403 from code-scanning API,
  CodeQL: 'Advanced Security must be enabled for this repository')

pr-validation.yml fixes:
- build-and-test: mvn jacoco:check -> jacoco:check@check (the
  default-cli goal cannot find pom.xml rules without the @check exec ID)
- build-and-test: add pull-requests: write permission (publish-unit-test-
  result-action gets 403 without it)
- secrets-scan: replace gitleaks/gitleaks-action@v2 (requires paid
  GITLEAKS_LICENSE for org repos) with trufflesecurity/trufflehog@v3.93.7
  (free OSS, no license needed for org repos); remove continue-on-error
- dependency-review: remove continue-on-error: true (wrong workaround
  from previous session; job correctly fails until GHAS is enabled)

ci.yml fixes:
- security-gate: add continue-on-error: true to upload-sarif step only
  (OWASP scan itself does not need GHAS; only the SARIF upload to GitHub
  Security does; scan report is still uploaded as Actions artifact)

Remaining: codeql and dependency-review will fail until GHAS is enabled
at https://github.com/paloitmbb/mbb-java-maven/settings/security_analysis

commit.message:
fix: fix slides after #show/#set rules not rendering subsequent slides (#268)

* Initial plan

* fix: correctly handle styled nodes containing subsequent slide headings

When a `#show` or `#set` rule creates a `styled` node whose inner content
contains headings that break into new slides, the rendered new slides were
previously wrapped in `_delayed-wrapper`. This wrapper only shows content on
the last subslide, so when the Second Slide had only one subslide, the Third
Slide's content was never rendered.

The fix: when the recursive `split-content-into-slides` call on a styled
node's inner content returns non-none `slide-content-part` (meaning new
slides were found), add the pre-heading content to the current slide, flush
the current slide, then push the new slides directly to `output-slides`
instead of wrapping them in `_delayed-wrapper`.

When no new slides are found (`slide-content-part == none`), the original
`_delayed-wrapper` behaviour is preserved for correct subslide animations.

Fixes: slides after a table with `#show table.cell: it => {}` not rendering

Co-authored-by: OrangeX4 <34951714+OrangeX4@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
Add per-bone is_kinematic flag to pipeline design

Kinematic bones skip Euler-Verlet integration entirely — they are
purely animation-driven (inv_mass=0, no external forces). Dynamic
bones get full integration where animation advances the interpolation
target and external forces apply on top.

The flag is a Blender custom property per bone, exported in the fskel
COLL chunk as part of bone_collider_desc_t.

commit.message:
feat(skill-eval): deterministic + LLM-as-judge eval harness for SF skills

SOURCE: philschmid.de/testing-skills (public article)
WHY: SF had 60+ skills shipped without systematic evals — the "vibe-checked
with a handful of manual runs, then shipped" anti-pattern. SkillsBench
(arxiv:2602.12670) counted 47k+ skills across GitHub with near-zero eval
coverage. We port the article's full harness approach.

## platform/tools/skill_eval_tools.py (eval engine)
- Deterministic checks: regex/not_regex/length_min/has_keyword/has_section/no_placeholder
  (fast, zero LLM cost — article: "deterministic checks are fast")
- LLM-as-judge: structured JSON output for qualitative expectations
  (article: "use structured output to constrain the LLM's response to a typed schema")
- Multi-trial: N runs per case, report distribution (article: "run 3-5 trials")
- 3 dimensions: outcome (works?), style (conventions?), efficiency (tokens)
- Negative tests: should_trigger:false cases prevent over-broad skill descriptions
- Retirement detection: if skill passes without being loaded → retire it
- Pass rate ≥80% = ready to ship (article graduation rule)
- Results persisted to data/skill_evals/<skill>.json

## platform/web/routes/api/skill_eval.py (REST API)
- GET /api/skills/eval — coverage summary (total, %, passing, gaps)
- GET /api/skills/eval/{name} — full last result per skill
- POST /api/skills/eval/{name}/run — async trigger with job_id
- GET /api/skills/eval/job/{job_id} — poll job status
- GET /api/skills/list — all skills with eval coverage metadata

## /art — 5th tab "Skills Health"
- Coverage progress bar (% of skills with eval_cases)
- Table: skills sorted by pass rate (green ≥80%, yellow ≥60%, red <60%)
- Panel: skills missing eval_cases (named list)
- Graduation tip + link to skill-eval workflow

## sf CLI: sf skills eval + sf skills coverage
- sf skills eval <name> — run eval harness, show per-case breakdown
- sf skills eval --all — run all skills with eval_cases
- sf skills eval <name> --trials=5 — override trial count
- sf skills coverage — quick coverage snapshot

## eval_cases added to 4 skills (3 cases each, with checks + expectations)
- skills/tdd.md — tdd-new-feature, tdd-bug-regression, tdd-no-trigger-doc
- skills/code-review.md — review-with-bug (SQL injection), clean-code, perf
- skills/error-monitoring.md — classify-new, classify-regression, ongoing-suppress
- skills/clean-code.md — no-stub-allowed, no-mock-data, edge-case-handled
Coverage: 10/57 skills (18%) → foundation for iterating to 100%

Co-authored-by: Copilot <223556219+Copilot@users.noreply.github.com>

commit.message:
refactor: change ov_bitarray from size_t to uint8_t

Eliminates endianness issues when casting bitarray pointers to
uint8_t* for base64 serialization. The previous size_t-based
implementation produced different byte sequences on different
platforms when raw memory was serialized.

Changes:
- typedef: size_t -> uint8_t
- Simplified set/get/clear inline functions (no more word/bit split)
- length_to_bytes: now just (bits + 7) / 8
- OV_ARRAY_HEADER macro: cast through void* to suppress -Wcast-align
  warning for uint8_t* arrays (actual alignment is guaranteed by allocator)

Fix constraint solver FK ordering + create unified pipeline tickets

Split constraint_solver_evaluate into 2 phases:
- Phase 1: FK propagation for ALL bones (world poses from local)
- Phase 2: Constraint evaluation in skeleton order

humanoid skeleton read from higher-indexed bones that hadn't been
computed yet (got stale/zero data).

- rpg-rcii: Joint metadata export in fskel format

- rpg-sqf8: Walk cycle visual test validation
- rpg-x7fe: Joint physical properties (damping/elasticity/yield/break)

fix: also use discoveryrule.get in get_template_full for diff/plan path

The plan/diff command uses get_template_full which also had selectDiscoveryRules
in template.get — same bug: returns empty on Zabbix 6.4/7.0.

Switch to discoveryrule.get with templateids + selectItems + selectTriggers

based on dashboard context. Now, permission is granted if the report is

<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

> 
> ----
> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>Refactor configuration handling via Functional Options
(Dependency Injection)</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> Currently, the `manael` package reads environment variables directly
within its core logic (e.g., `os.Getenv("MANAEL_ENABLE_AVIF")` in
`avifEnabled()`).
> For a modern Go library design, a package should not implicitly rely
on environment variables. Instead, the application entry point
(`cmd/manael/main.go`) should be responsible for reading environment
variables and passing those configurations down to the library.
> 
> ### Proposal
> Introduce an `Options` struct and use the **Functional Options
pattern** to pass configurations to `NewServeProxy()`. This separates
the concerns, makes the library cleaner, and significantly improves
testability since we won't need to mock environment variables inside the
package logic.
> 
> ### Example Implementation
> ```go
> // manael.go
> type ProxyOptions struct {
> 	EnableAVIF bool
> }
> 
> type ProxyOption func(*ProxyOptions)
> 
> func WithAVIFEnabled(enabled bool) ProxyOption {
> 	return func(o *ProxyOptions) {
> 		o.EnableAVIF = enabled
> 	}
> }
> 
> // Update the constructor to accept options
> func NewServeProxy(u *url.URL, opts ...ProxyOption) http.Handler {
> 	options := &ProxyOptions{}
> 	for _, o := range opts {
> 		o(options)
> 	}
> 
> 	return &httputil.ReverseProxy{
> 		Rewrite: func(r *httputil.ProxyRequest) {
> 			// ...
> 		},
> 		ModifyResponse: func(res *http.Response) error {
> 			return modifyResponse(res, options)
> 		},
> 	}
> }
> 
> func avifEnabled(res *http.Response, opts *ProxyOptions) bool {
> 	contentType := res.Header.Get("Content-Type")
> return opts.EnableAVIF && contentType != "image/png" && contentType !=
"image/gif"
> }
> ```
> 
> ```go
> // cmd/manael/main.go
> func main() {
> 	// ...
> 	enableAVIF := os.Getenv("MANAEL_ENABLE_AVIF") == "true"
> 	
> handler := manael.NewServeProxy(upstreamURL,
manael.WithAVIFEnabled(enableAVIF))
> 	// ...
> }
> ```</issue_description>

commit.message:
ci: support multi-architecture Docker builds (linux/amd64, linux/arm64) (#1626)

Manael's Docker image was only built for `amd64`, leaving ARM-based
deployments (AWS Graviton, Apple Silicon) without native images.

## Changes

- **`build.yml`**: Added `platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64` to the
`docker/build-push-action` step — the QEMU and Buildx setup steps were
already present but the platform target was never specified
- **`build.yml`**: Fixed a mislabeled step: "Set up QEMU" was
incorrectly using `actions/setup-go`; renamed it to "Set up Go"

```yaml
- name: Build and push
  uses: docker/build-push-action@...
  with:
    platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
    push: ${{ github.event_name == 'release' }}
    # ...
```

> Note: QEMU-based `arm64` builds with CGO/libvips will increase CI
build time significantly for release jobs — this is an expected
tradeoff.

<details>

<summary>Original prompt</summary>

>

> 
> *This section details on the original issue you should resolve*
> 
> <issue_title>CI: Support multi-architecture Docker builds
(linux/amd64, linux/arm64)</issue_title>
> <issue_description>### Background
> With the increasing adoption of ARM-based cloud infrastructure (such
as AWS Graviton) and Apple Silicon (M-series Macs) for local
development, providing only an `amd64` Docker image is no longer
sufficient. Image processing is a CPU-bound task, and many users want to
deploy Manael on cost-effective `arm64` servers.
> 
> ### Proposal
> Update the GitHub Actions workflow to build and push
multi-architecture Docker images (`linux/amd64` and `linux/arm64`) to
the GitHub Container Registry (GHCR) and/or Docker Hub.
> 
> Since Manael v3.0 relies on CGO and `libvips`, we cannot simply
cross-compile with `GOARCH=arm64`. Instead, we should use Docker Buildx
with QEMU to emulate the target architecture during the build process.
> 
> ### Tasks
> - Update the Docker build workflow (e.g.,
`.github/workflows/docker.yml`).
> - Add `docker/setup-qemu-action` to enable emulation.
> - Add `docker/setup-buildx-action` to enable Buildx.
> - Modify the `docker/build-push-action` step to specify `platforms:
linux/amd64,linux/arm64`.
> 
> ### Example Workflow Snippet
> ```yaml
> jobs:
>   docker:
>     runs-on: ubuntu-latest
>     steps:
>       - name: Checkout
>         uses: actions/checkout@v4
> 
>       - name: Set up QEMU
>         uses: docker/setup-qemu-action@v3
> 
>       - name: Set up Docker Buildx
>         uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
> 
>       - name: Login to GHCR
>         uses: docker/login-action@v3
>         with:
>           registry: ghcr.io
>           username: ${{ github.repository_owner }}
>           password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
> 
>       - name: Build and push
>         uses: docker/build-push-action@v5
>         with:
>           context: .
>           platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64
>           push: true
> tags:
ghcr.io/manaelproxy/manael:latest,ghcr.io/manaelproxy/manael:v3.0.0
> ```
> 
> ### Note on Build Times
> Using QEMU for building CGO and installing C-dependencies (`libvips`)
will significantly increase the CI build time for the `arm64` target.
This is a known tradeoff and perfectly acceptable for release
builds.</issue_description>
>

test: add native Go fuzz tests for header parsing and content negotiation (#1621)

Untrusted HTTP header inputs (e.g. `Accept`, `Vary`, `Cache-Control`)
feed directly into parsing logic that could panic on malformed edge
cases. Go 1.18+ native fuzzing lets the toolchain explore these paths
automatically.

## Changes

- **`fuzz_test.go`** (new, `package manael` internal test package to
access unexported functions):
- `FuzzScanAcceptHeader` — arbitrary `Accept` header values through
`scanAcceptHeader`
- `FuzzCheck` — arbitrary method, status code, `Content-Type`,
`Cache-Control`, and `Accept` through `check`; uses
`&http.Request{Method: method}` directly instead of
`httptest.NewRequest` to avoid panicking on invalid HTTP method tokens
before reaching the code under test
- `FuzzIsAPNG` — arbitrary byte sequences through `isAPNG` (empty,
non-PNG, truncated, partial chunks)
- `FuzzIsAnimatedGIF` — arbitrary byte sequences through `isAnimatedGIF`
(empty, non-GIF, minimal LSD, trailer-only, single-frame)
- `FuzzSetVaryHeader` — arbitrary `Vary` values through `setVaryHeader`,
asserting the output `Vary` header is never empty

Each function ships a seed corpus covering real-world inputs, so `go
test` runs the seeds without needing `-fuzz`.

```go
func FuzzIsAPNG(f *testing.F) {
    f.Add([]byte{0x89, 0x50, 0x4E, 0x47, 0x0D, 0x0A, 0x1A, 0x0A}) // valid PNG sig
    f.Add([]byte{})
    f.Add([]byte("GIF89a"))

    f.Fuzz(func(t *testing.T, data []byte) {
        _, _ = isAPNG(bytes.NewReader(data))
    })
}
```

Run with extended fuzzing via:
```
go test -fuzz=FuzzScanAcceptHeader -fuzztime=60s
```

commit.message:
feat: async/await support with yield-from semantics (#38)

* feat: async/await support with yield-from semantics

Add async function compilation to coroutine objects that work with
MicroPython's asyncio event loop.

Key components:
- AsyncEmitter: extends GeneratorEmitter for coroutine code generation
- AwaitIR and AwaitModuleCallIR: IR nodes for await expressions
- Coroutine type with __await__, send, throw, close methods
- Proper yield-from pattern for await (iterate awaitable to completion)

The critical fix: await expressions now use mp_iternext() to drive
awaitables in a loop until MP_OBJ_STOP_ITERATION, capturing the result
from MP_STATE_THREAD(stop_iteration_arg). This correctly handles:
- Awaitables that yield multiple times
- Return value propagation from sub-coroutines
- Multiple sequential awaits in one function

Tested on ESP32-C6 device with asyncio.run() - all 396 device tests pass.

* fix: resolve lint errors (import order, trailing whitespace)

* fix: address Copilot review comments

- Fix mp_make_stop_iteration to store value in _mock_stop_iteration_arg
- Use per-object tracking for async iterator mock instead of global counter
- Remove duplicate _emit_wrapper_signature (inherits from GeneratorEmitter)
- Fix missing comma in coroutine type table generation
- Add blank lines for code style consistency (ir.py, test_compiler.py)
- Update docstrings to match actual implementation (async_demo.py, ir.py)
- Move async/await section out of 'Out-of-Scope' in docs
- Fix blog examples to match actual code (countdown returns int, not str)

* feat: add yield from support to generators

- Add YieldFromIR dataclass for yield from statements
- Implement yield from emission using mp_getiter/mp_iternext pattern
- Add _yield_iter field to generator struct for sub-iterator tracking
- Update IR builder to handle ast.YieldFrom in statements
- Add IR visualization support for YieldFromIR

Examples added:
- delegate_to_list: simple delegation to list
- flatten: nested list flattening with recursive yield from
- chain_iterables: chaining two iterables
- prefix_and_delegate: mixing yield and yield from

Tests: 4 unit tests, 3 C runtime tests, 4 device tests
Benchmarks: yield from is 1.5-1.6x faster than vanilla MicroPython

Also fixes benchmark runner to use mpremote (was incorrectly using python3).

* fix: address second round of Copilot review comments

- Simplify throw method by removing redundant conditional branches
- Add AsyncFunctionDef to body_ast type hint for accuracy
- Update AwaitIR docstring to clarify simple vs yield-from patterns
- Add blank line before summary section in device tests

* Update docs/05-roadmap.md
